headline: DOE chief charged with taking RM100K to bypass an EIA — on a road that killed nine
context: Malaysia's Environment DG and Deputy DG face corruption charges (April 2026). The DG allegedly took RM100,000 for an EIA exemption on Penang's Bukit Kukus road — where a 2018 landslide killed nine foreign workers.

cards.big: Malaysia's top two environmental regulators are charged — the DG for taking RM100,000 to consider an EIA bypass.
cards.sub: The deputy allegedly took payments through a child's account from a waste firm she regulated. The road's victims were Bangladeshi, Indonesian and Myanmar workers.

cards.big: The DG asked for 3,000 more pollution-control staff. Fourteen months later he allegedly took RM100,000 cash at his office.
cards.sub: DOE runs 1,100 officers for 60,000 facilities — a 1:55 ratio. Singapore operates at 1:15. E-waste exemption power sat at the DG's personal discretion.

cards.big: E-waste smuggling ran five years across Customs, ports, police and DOE — only the DG's arrest triggered a nationwide import ban.
cards.sub: RM17.4M frozen across two raids. Ban announced Feb 4, gazetted Apr 1. 800+ containers stranded at Port Klang. The regulator was one compromised node in a longer chain.

cards.big: The DG faces 20 years under the MACC Act, the deputy 2 years under the Penal Code. No audit of past approvals has been announced.
cards.sub: Payments to the deputy ran two years. Six e-waste owners face separate proceedings. The approvals these officials signed remain unreviewed.

cards.big: The nine dead at Bukit Kukus were foreign workers with no voice in the EIA. Communities near e-waste sites bear health costs no one measures.
cards.sub: B40 and Orang Asli communities live beside unregulated waste with no health monitoring. Every coalition has shielded the regulator.

cards.big: Like learning the building inspector took bribes to skip the fire exits — after the building already burned down.
cards.sub: The charges don't answer how many other approvals were compromised, or whether Sabah and Sarawak face the same risk.

cards.big: The fix is independent environmental courts and community standing to challenge approvals — not more staff for a captured regulator.
